program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Reading a market outlook and planning for each scenario -- the first conditional (if + present, will + base) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- The Market Outlook -- Conditions, Scenarios and the First Conditional
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 8 -- The Market Outlook

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, this is Paul, your market analyst, with the outlook before Thursday. The headline is simple: the market is strong but volatile. Here is how I read it. If demand keeps rising the way it has this quarter, you will need more capacity by the summer, so plan for it now. If a large competitor enters your segment, and I think one will, do not compete on price. As long as your quality and your lead time stay ahead, you will keep your margin and your market share. One warning: if a downturn hits, it will hit fast, so keep a contingency ready. But unless the whole sector collapses, the trend is up. If you diversify now, you will be in a strong position next year. Talk on Thursday.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Rachel, from the board, with a few thoughts before the call. I have read Paul's outlook, and I agree with most of it. The board's view is this: if the new sensor line performs, we will back a full launch and support you breaking into North America. If it underperforms, we will not panic; we will slow down and protect the margin. On competition, we trust your instinct. If a bigger player moves in, we will compete on quality, exactly as you said. And please keep diversifying. As long as the core business is healthy, every new product family reduces our risk. One last thing: if you ever need more capital for capacity, ask early. We would rather plan than react. See you Thursday.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the maximum a company can produce
how much customers want to buy a product
a company that sells similar products in the same market
the time between ordering and delivering
a period when business or the economy gets weaker
to add new products or markets to reduce risk

paras: The market for wireless components is growing fast, but it is also volatile. Demand can surge in one quarter and cool the next. At AVRT, the plan for next year depends on a few clear conditions. If demand keeps rising, the company will expand its capacity and break into the North American market more aggressively. If it slows, AVRT will hold back and protect its margin instead.

Competition is another factor. A larger competitor is expected to enter the same segment, and that could be a real disruption. 'If a big player moves in, we will not compete on price,' Izabel says. 'We will compete on quality and lead time.' To reduce risk, AVRT will diversify: as long as the core sensor line stays strong, the company will add two new product families and grow its market share step by step.

Nothing is left to chance. If a downturn hits, a contingency plan is ready: costs will be cut, and the launch will be postponed rather than cancelled. 'Unless the whole market collapses, we will keep investing in research,' Izabel explains. 'If we stop innovating, we will lose the very thing that makes us different.' The message to the board is simple: the future is uncertain, but every likely scenario has a plan.
reading.source: Adapted from a company strategy brief, The Market Outlook Review

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: AVRT has decided to leave the wireless components market for good.
AVRT has a clear plan for each likely market scenario, and competes on quality rather than price.
AVRT will lower its prices to beat every competitor.

items: If demand keeps rising, AVRT will expand its capacity.
'If demand keeps rising, the company will expand its capacity and break into the North American market.'
If a big competitor enters, AVRT will compete mainly on price.
'If a big player moves in, we will not compete on price... We will compete on quality and lead time.'
AVRT plans to diversify by adding new product families.
'the company will add two new product families and grow its market share.'
If a downturn hits, AVRT will cancel the launch.
'the launch will be postponed rather than cancelled.'

parts: At AVRT, the plan depends on the market. If demand
its capacity. If a large competitor
on quality, not price. If a downturn

gapfill.text: First conditional = if + present simple, ... will + base verb. The 'if' part uses the PRESENT, never 'will': If demand rises (not 'if demand will rise'), we will expand. Use 'unless' for 'if not', and 'as long as' for a condition that must stay true.

bank.label: Useful language for talking about scenarios
items: If demand rises, we will expand our capacity.
If a competitor enters, we will compete on quality.
Unless the market collapses, we will keep investing.
As long as the core line stays strong, we will diversify.
If costs go up, our margin will fall.
If we win the contract, we will break into a new market.

lf.title: First conditional: which form?
items: If we launch in Q3, revenue
Unless we act now, a competitor
As long as the core line stays strong, we

lf.text: First conditional: the 'if / unless / as long as' part uses the present simple; the result uses will + base verb. Never put 'will' after 'if'. Use 'may' or 'might' in the result if it is only possible: If demand cools, we might postpone the launch.

items: The board asks what happens if demand rises next year. Answer with a first conditional.
Explain what AVRT will do if a large competitor enters the market.
A partner worries about a downturn. Describe your contingency using 'if' and 'unless'.
Describe one decision in your own work that depends on a condition, using the first conditional.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = rises, 2 = will expand, 3 = enters, 4 = will compete, 5 = hits, 6 = will be postponed.
Demand: 'If demand rises, we will expand our capacity and break into North America.'
Competitor: 'If a big player enters, we will not compete on price; we will compete on quality and lead time.'
Downturn: 'Unless the market collapses, we will keep investing; if a downturn hits, costs will be cut and the launch postponed.'
answerkey.note: First conditional = if + present simple, will + base. Use 'unless' for 'if not', and 'as long as / provided that' for a condition that must stay true.
